direction: Using a pre-staged California dataset at MSSA (Medical Service Study Area) level, determine whether PROXIMITY TO THE PACIFIC OCEAN explains any of the geographic variation in age-adjusted cancer incidence (AAIR) once POLLUTION is controlled for. This is the operator's Project Description: prune to low-pollution / similar-pollution MSSAs (using CalEnviroScreen 4.0 metrics - composite pollution burden plus components such as PM2.5, ozone, diesel, traffic, toxic release, hazardous waste, drinking-water and cleanup-site indicators) and then compare cancer rates against distance from the ocean, optionally binarized to 'close to ocean' vs 'far from ocean'. Do this for ALL-cancer (the AllSite site) and for EACH of the 13 cancer sites separately. Quantify the contribution of distance-to-ocean two complementary ways: (1) XGBoost regression of AAIR on distance_to_ocean_km plus the pollution and demographic covariates, with SHAP values and permutation importance to isolate how much distance contributes over and above pollution; and (2) classical multivariable regression (OLS/WLS, weighted toward MSSAs with stable rates) reporting the distance coefficient, its 95% confidence interval and p-value, both crude and pollution-adjusted, plus the stratified low-pollution-subset comparison the Project Description asks for. The honest expected finding is that any raw coastal-vs-inland incidence gap is largely confounded by urbanicity, smoking, socioeconomic status, screening access and pollution; the scientific contribution is to measure how much of the distance signal SURVIVES adjustment, for which cancers, and in which direction. Treat this strictly as an ECOLOGICAL association study (area-level, not individual-level), report a null or attenuated effect as a legitimate result, and flag any cancer where distance remains a strong predictor after full adjustment as a counterintuitive finding requiring interpretation.

context.origin: Operator-provided real-data study (2026-06-24) from California Health Maps (cancer incidence by MSSA) + CalEnviroScreen 4.0 (pollution). Data are already merged and staged locally; a geographic exposure (distance_to_ocean_km) has been derived by the operator. The pipeline performs the statistics, not the data engineering.

constraints.compute: Pure CPU, scikit-learn / xgboost / statsmodels / shap (all pre-installed). No GPU required. No network. Full analysis across 13 cancers should target < 30 minutes wall-clock.
constraints.data_is_prestaged_local_csv: Read the ABSOLUTE path /home/gdp/autoscientist/projects/california-cancer-ocean/data/analysis_dataset.csv with pandas. DO NOT call dataset_fetch/dataset_info. DO NOT download anything. The companion data/data_dictionary.md documents every column.
constraints.do_not_recompute_geography: distance_to_ocean_km, coastal_20km, coastal_50km and dist_band are ALREADY in the file (derived from MSSA centroids vs the California coastline). Use them as-is; do NOT attempt to recompute distances or fetch shapefiles.
constraints.unit_of_analysis: MSSA (542 areas). Ecological design - interpret at the area level only.
constraints.outcome: AAIR (age-adjusted incidence rate per 100,000), 10-year window. LCI/UCI give the rate's 95% CI; PopTot is the underlying population.
constraints.primary_exposure: distance_to_ocean_km (continuous). Secondary forms: coastal_20km, coastal_50km, dist_band.
constraints.cv: 5-fold; ALL preprocessing (imputation, scaling) fit on the training fold only via an sklearn Pipeline
constraints.external_validation: geographic hold-out (e.g., train Southern CA, test Northern CA) to check the distance signal generalizes spatially

context.ceiling: Workshop track / short methods note: an honest ecological analysis of distance-to-ocean and California cancer incidence with explicit pollution and confounder adjustment. Not a causal-inference flagship. Per KICKOFF.md Section 1.
hard_rules: this is an ECOLOGICAL study: never state or imply individual-level causation; phrase findings as area-level associations and name the ecological fallacy explicitly

report the distance-to-ocean effect BOTH crude and fully adjusted (pollution + smoking + SES + screening + urbanicity); the headline is how much of the crude coastal-vs-inland gap survives adjustment, per cancer
implement the Project Description's design directly: prune to low / similar-pollution MSSAs and re-test distance within that restricted, pollution-matched set
exclude leakage / definitional columns from the feature set (LCI/UCI, race-stratified AAIRs, post-hoc CES health outcomes like asthma/cardiovascular, raw lat/lon, identifiers)
AAIR is missing (small-count suppression) for ~16% of rows and more for rarer sites; disclose missingness per cancer and handle it explicitly - do not silently drop without reporting
weight or down-weight statistically unstable rates (wide LCI-UCI / small PopTot); a raw unweighted mean over MSSAs over-counts tiny, noisy areas
>= 5 seeds for XGBoost with mean +/- SD, and bootstrap or analytic 95% confidence intervals for every reported distance effect and cross-group comparison
all preprocessing fit on the training fold only (sklearn Pipeline); tune any XGBoost hyperparameters on a validation split disjoint from the test fold
acknowledge and, where feasible, test for spatial autocorrelation - neighbouring MSSAs are not independent, which inflates naive significance

run the analysis for all 13 cancer sites (incl. AllSite) and produce a single comparison table of the adjusted distance effect across sites; flag any site where distance stays a strong predictor after adjustment for interpretation
report sensitivity to the distance operationalization (continuous km vs 20km/50km binary vs bands) before claiming the result is robust
